title: Reverse LL in Groups of K

statement.markdown: Given a linked list, reverse the nodes of the list in groups of k. If the number of nodes is not a multiple of k, then the last group should remain as it is (i.e., not reversed). The input linked list is represented as a sequence of space-separated integers, and the output should also be a sequence of space-separated integers. The first line of the input contains the number of nodes in the linked list, followed by the node values. The second line contains the value of k. Your task is to write a program that takes this input and produces the reversed linked list in groups of k as output.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer n, the number of nodes in the linked list. The second line contains n space-separated integers representing the node values. The third line contains an integer k, the group size for reversal.
statement.outputFormat: A sequence of space-separated integers representing the node values of the reversed linked list in groups of k.
statement.notes: The input linked list is 1-indexed, and the node values are integers.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the linked list
The values of the nodes in the linked list

examples.explanation: Reverse the first 3 nodes (1, 2, 3) to get (3, 2, 1), and then append the remaining nodes (4, 5) as they are.
Reverse the first 2 nodes (1, 2) to get (2, 1), and then reverse the next 2 nodes (3, 4) to get (4, 3).

hints.text: Consider creating a helper function to reverse a linked list in-place. This will be useful for reversing each group of k nodes.
Think about how you can iterate over the linked list in groups of k nodes. You can use a counter to keep track of the current group size.
When you encounter a group of k nodes, you can reverse this group by calling your helper function. Remember to update the next pointers of the nodes accordingly.
After reversing each group, you need to update the next pointer of the last node in the previous group to point to the first node of the current group. This will link the groups together.
The key insight is to realize that you don't need to reverse the entire linked list. You only need to reverse each group of k nodes in-place and link them together.

editorial.title: Reversing a Linked List in Groups of K
editorial.intuition: The problem requires reversing a linked list in groups of K nodes. A brute force approach would involve reversing the entire list and then adjusting the pointers, but this would be inefficient. A key observation is that we can solve this problem recursively by reversing the first K nodes and then connecting the rest of the list to the reversed group.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve reversing the entire linked list and then trying to group the nodes into sets of K. This would be slow because it would require extra work to reconnect the nodes into groups. The time complexity of this approach would be much higher than the optimal solution.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach involves using a recursive strategy to reverse the linked list in groups of K. This approach takes advantage of the fact that we can reverse a linked list by changing the next pointers of the nodes.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves a recursive function that takes the head of the list and the group size K as input. It reverses the first K nodes and then connects the rest of the list to the reversed group. This approach has a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(1).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by induction. The base case is when the list has less than K nodes, in which case the list is returned as is. The inductive step involves reversing the first K nodes and then connecting the rest of the list to the reversed group. This process is repeated until the entire list is reversed in groups of K.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the optimal solution with a sample input can help verify its correctness. For example, if the input is a list of 6 nodes (1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5 -> 6) and K = 3, the solution should output the list 3 -> 2 -> 1 -> 6 -> 5 -> 4.
commonMistakes: Not handling the case where the list has less than K nodes
Not updating the next pointers correctly when reversing the list
Not connecting the rest of the list to the reversed group correctly
Using a non-recursive approach that requires extra space
Not considering the edge case where K is 1
Not verifying the correctness of the solution with a dry run
